PHYSICS: Newton's second law F=ma relates net force, mass, and acceleration in an inertial reference frame.
The Schrodinger equation governs quantum wavefunction time evolution.
Heisenberg uncertainty principle: delta_x * delta_p >= hbar/2 sets fundamental measurement limits.
Maxwell's equations unify electricity, magnetism, and light into electromagnetic field theory.
Special relativity: time dilation t' = gamma*t where gamma = 1/sqrt(1-v^2/c^2).
General relativity models gravity as spacetime curvature via Einstein's field equations.
First law of thermodynamics: internal energy change = heat added minus work done.
Entropy S = k_B * ln(Omega) where Omega is the number of accessible microstates.
Standard Model classifies quarks, leptons, gauge bosons, and the Higgs boson.
Superconductivity occurs when Cooper pairs form via phonon-mediated electron attraction.
Gravitational waves are spacetime ripples at c, first detected by LIGO in 2015.
Einstein's photoelectric effect E=hv demonstrated light's particle nature.
Black hole event horizons occur at Schwarzschild radius r_s = 2GM/c^2.
Pauli exclusion principle forbids two identical fermions from sharing a quantum state.
Boltzmann distribution P(E) proportional to exp(-E/k_BT) gives thermal equilibrium probabilities.
Doppler shift changes observed frequency; applies in radar, sonar, and cosmology.
Wave-particle duality: every quantum entity exhibits both interference and discrete detection.
Lagrangian mechanics uses generalized coordinates and the principle of least action.
Gauss's law: electric flux through a closed surface equals enclosed charge divided by epsilon_0.
The Carnot cycle sets the maximum efficiency for any reversible heat engine.
CHEMISTRY: The periodic table organizes elements by atomic number, revealing trends in electronegativity and radius.
Covalent bonds form when atoms share electron pairs; bond strength correlates with bond order.
Le Chatelier's principle: a system at equilibrium shifts to oppose any imposed stress.
Gibbs free energy deltaG = deltaH - T*deltaS determines spontaneity; deltaG < 0 means spontaneous.
Arrhenius equation k = A*exp(-Ea/RT) shows rate constants increase exponentially with temperature.
Strong acids fully dissociate in water; weak acids establish equilibrium described by Ka.
SN2 reactions proceed via backside attack with inversion of configuration at the carbon center.
Chirality produces enantiomers with identical physical properties but opposite optical rotation.
Transition metals form complexes via coordinate bonds; crystal field theory explains their colors.
Henderson-Hasselbalch equation: pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]) governs buffer solutions.
NMR spectroscopy probes molecular structure through nuclear spin energy differences.
Catalysts lower activation energy without being consumed; enzymes achieve rate enhancements of 10^6 to 10^12.
Electrons occupy s, p, d, f orbitals derived from solving the Schrodinger equation.
Colligative properties depend on solute particle concentration, not identity.
Electronegativity differences greater than 1.7 produce ionic bonds; less than 0.4 produce nonpolar covalent bonds.
Polymers form by addition or condensation polymerization of repeat monomer units.
Oxidation loses electrons; reduction gains electrons (OIL RIG).
Entropy drives dissolution: delta_S_mix > 0 when solute disperses among solvent molecules.
Thermite reaction Fe2O3 + 2Al yields Al2O3 + 2Fe releasing about 850 kJ/mol.
Wavefunctions squared give electron probability density in the quantum model of the atom.
BIOLOGY: DNA encodes genetic information as base-pair sequences: A pairs with T, G pairs with C.
Central dogma: DNA to RNA (transcription) to protein (translation), with exceptions in retroviruses.
Natural selection acts on heritable variation; higher fitness leads to more offspring.
ATP hydrolysis releases about 30.5 kJ/mol; it powers cellular work across all life forms.
Photosynthesis: 6CO2 + 6H2O + light yields glucose + 6O2 via the Calvin cycle.
Mitosis produces two identical daughter cells; meiosis produces four haploid gametes.
B cells produce antibodies; T cells mediate cellular immunity against pathogens.
Protein structure: primary (sequence), secondary (helices/sheets), tertiary (3D fold), quaternary (subunits).
CRISPR-Cas9 uses guide RNA to direct Cas9 nuclease to specific DNA sequences for precise editing.
Phylogenetic trees represent evolutionary relationships inferred from molecular and morphological data.
Action potentials propagate via Na+ influx (depolarization) followed by K+ efflux (repolarization).
Cell cycle G1, S (DNA replication), G2, M phases are regulated by cyclin-CDK complexes.
Epigenetics: heritable gene expression changes occur via DNA methylation and histone modification.
Oncogenes promote unregulated growth; tumor suppressors like p53 halt proliferation at DNA damage.
Neurotransmitters cross synaptic clefts; reuptake terminates the signal.
Competitive exclusion principle: two species cannot occupy exactly the same ecological niche.
Hormones bind receptors to trigger cellular responses; insulin lowers blood glucose via GLUT4.
Evolutionary convergence produces similar traits independently under similar selection pressures.
Ribosomes translate mRNA codons to amino acids using tRNA anticodons.
Immune memory enables faster, stronger responses to repeat infections.
MATHEMATICS: Fundamental theorem of calculus links differentiation and integration.
Euclid's algorithm computes GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b) in O(log min(a,b)) steps.
A basis is a minimal spanning set; every vector has a unique basis decomposition.
Godel's incompleteness theorems: any sufficiently powerful consistent system contains unprovable truths.
Euler's identity: e^(i*pi) + 1 = 0 unifies five fundamental mathematical constants.
Prime number theorem: pi(n) approximately n/ln(n) describes asymptotic prime density.
Fourier analysis decomposes periodic functions into sums of sinusoids; fundamental to signal processing.
Central limit theorem: sums of independent random variables converge to a normal distribution.
Bayes theorem: P(A|B) = P(B|A)*P(A)/P(B) updates beliefs given new evidence.
Euler's formula for planar graphs: V - E + F = 2.
The Riemann hypothesis conjectures all nontrivial zeros of the zeta function lie on Re(s) = 1/2.
Matrix multiplication is associative but not commutative; det(AB) = det(A)*det(B).
Topology: a coffee cup and a donut are topologically equivalent as genus-1 surfaces.
ZFC set theory provides the axiomatic foundation for modern mathematics.
The Euler-Lagrange equation solves optimization problems over function spaces.
Cauchy's integral theorem: the integral of an analytic function over a closed contour is zero.
Linear ODEs have closed-form solutions via integrating factors or eigenvalue methods.
Group theory: a group is closed under an associative operation with identity and inverses.
The Pythagorean theorem a^2 + b^2 = c^2 holds for all right triangles in Euclidean geometry.
Fermat's last theorem proved by Wiles in 1995: no integers satisfy a^n + b^n = c^n for n > 2.
EARTH SCIENCES: Plate tectonics: rigid lithospheric plates move over the asthenosphere at centimeters per year.
Radiometric dating uses known decay rates (carbon-14 half-life 5730 years) to date materials.
Earth's outer core is liquid iron-nickel generating the geomagnetic field via dynamo action.
Greenhouse effect: CO2, H2O, CH4 absorb infrared radiation, warming Earth's surface.
S-waves are blocked by Earth's liquid outer core; P-waves travel through solids and liquids.
Ocean thermohaline circulation is driven by density differences from temperature and salinity.
Ice cores preserve 800000-year climate records in trapped air bubbles and isotope ratios.
The Richter scale is logarithmic; each unit equals about 31.6 times more seismic energy.
Soil horizon A (topsoil) is richest in organic matter; horizon B accumulates leached minerals.
High silica content produces viscous magma and explosive volcanic eruptions.
Atmospheric layers: troposphere (weather), stratosphere (ozone), mesosphere, thermosphere.
ENSO (El Nino-Southern Oscillation) disrupts global weather via Pacific SST anomalies.
CFCs catalytically destroy stratospheric ozone via halogen radical chain reactions.
Nitrogen cycle: fixation to nitrates to plant uptake to decomposition to denitrification back to N2.
Sea floor spreading at mid-ocean ridges records geomagnetic reversals in magnetic striping.
Tsunamis are generated by undersea earthquakes or landslides displacing large water volumes.
Aquifers store groundwater in porous rock; over-extraction causes land subsidence.
The rock cycle converts igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks through geological time.
Fossil fuels are ancient organic matter compressed over millions of years.
Glacial ice cores capture atmospheric composition data spanning 800000 years of climate history.
ASTRONOMY: The Hertzsprung-Russell diagram plots stellar luminosity vs. temperature, revealing stellar evolution stages.
Stars fuse hydrogen to helium via the pp chain or CNO cycle, releasing energy via E=mc^2.
The cosmic microwave background is thermal radiation from 380000 years after the Big Bang at 2.725 K.
Dark matter comprises about 27% of the universe's energy budget and interacts only gravitationally.
Hubble's law v = H0*d: galaxies recede at speeds proportional to distance, evidence of expansion.
Pulsars are rotating neutron stars emitting periodic radio beams; millisecond pulsars are stable clocks.
Type Ia supernovae are standard candles enabling distance measurement across cosmological scales.
Hawking radiation theoretically causes black holes to slowly evaporate over astronomical timescales.
One parsec equals 3.26 light-years, the distance at which Earth's orbit subtends 1 arcsecond.
The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy about 100000 light-years across containing about 300 billion stars.
Exoplanet transit photometry detects brightness dips as planets cross their host star.
Gravitational lensing bends light around massive objects, mapping dark matter distribution.
Quasars are extremely luminous active galactic nuclei powered by accretion onto supermassive black holes.
The observable universe spans about 93 billion light-years due to cosmic expansion.
Cepheid variable star periods are proportional to luminosity, providing a cosmic distance ladder rung.
James Webb Space Telescope observes in infrared and detected CO2 in the WASP-39b exoplanet atmosphere.
Solar wind shapes planetary magnetospheres; Earth's magnetosphere deflects most charged particles.
Galaxy mergers drive star formation; the Milky Way and Andromeda will collide in about 4.5 billion years.
Reionization occurred about 1 billion years after the Big Bang as first stars ionized neutral hydrogen.
Neutron star densities reach about 10^17 kg/m^3; a teaspoon would weigh about 1 billion tonnes.
CLINICAL MEDICINE: Evidence-based medicine integrates research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values.
Sepsis is life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response; early antibiotics are critical.
Myocardial infarction results from coronary occlusion; troponin is the primary diagnostic biomarker.
Glasgow Coma Scale (3 to 15) assesses eye opening, verbal response, and motor response.
Type 1 diabetes involves autoimmune beta-cell destruction; Type 2 involves insulin resistance.
Hypertension is diagnosed at sustained BP >= 130/80 mmHg; it is the leading modifiable stroke risk factor.
TNM staging classifies cancer by tumor size, node involvement, and metastasis.
Pharmacokinetics: ADME (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion) governs drug concentration.
Penicillins target bacterial cell wall synthesis; fluoroquinolones inhibit DNA gyrase.
Ischemic stroke accounts for 87% of strokes; FAST (Face, Arm, Speech, Time) aids recognition.
Asthma involves reversible bronchospasm; bronchodilators and inhaled corticosteroids are first-line.
Randomized controlled trials are the gold standard for treatment efficacy evaluation.
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) is defined as EF less than 40%.
mRNA vaccines encode antigen for host cell production, stimulating adaptive immunity.
MRI uses hydrogen nuclear spin relaxation; T1 and T2 differences create tissue contrast.
WHO pain ladder: non-opioids to weak opioids to strong opioids with adjuvants at each step.
Drug interactions alter pharmacokinetics via CYP450 enzyme induction or inhibition.
COPD is characterized by irreversible airflow limitation; FEV1/FVC ratio less than 0.70 confirms obstruction.
Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, reducing LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular event risk.
Surgical site infection is reduced by prophylactic antibiotics and sterile technique.
PREVENTIVE & PUBLIC HEALTH MEDICINE: Incidence measures new cases per time period; prevalence measures existing cases at a point in time.
Relative risk compares disease rates between exposed and unexposed groups; RR > 1 indicates increased risk.
Herd immunity interrupts transmission when sufficient population fraction is immune.
Social determinants of health account for 30 to 55% of health outcomes.
Bradford Hill criteria assess causation including strength, consistency, temporality, and biological gradient.
Global burden of disease uses DALYs combining years lost to premature death and disability.
The basic reproduction number R0 is the average secondary infections per case in a fully susceptible population.
Non-communicable diseases cause 74% of global deaths; smoking is the leading preventable cause.
Water sanitation and handwashing reduced cholera mortality by more than 99% in developed nations.
Mediterranean diet reduces cardiovascular risk by about 30% compared to typical Western diet.
Blood lead level greater than 5 micrograms per deciliter in children requires intervention.
Asbestos exposure causes mesothelioma after 20 to 50 year latency period.
PM2.5 air pollution causes about 7 million deaths per year; cardiovascular disease is the top linked cause.
Global maternal mortality ratio is 211 per 100000 live births.
Screening balances sensitivity (true positive rate) against specificity (true negative rate).
Measles requires about 95% vaccination coverage to achieve herd immunity.
Contact tracing identifies and notifies exposed persons, breaking transmission chains.
Health impact assessment predicts policy effects on population health distribution.
The precautionary principle justifies preventive action under uncertainty when risks are serious.
Occupational exposure limits define maximum permissible workplace contaminant concentrations.
ALLIED HEALTH: Physical therapy restores mobility using exercise, manual therapy, and modalities after injury.
Occupational therapy uses adaptive equipment and task modification to restore daily living skills.
Speech-language pathology addresses aphasia, dysarthria, dysphagia, and childhood language disorders.
Medical laboratory scientists perform complete blood counts, metabolic panels, cultures, and histopathology.
Pharmacists verify orders, counsel on adherence, and identify drug interactions.
Respiratory therapists manage mechanical ventilation and perform pulmonary function tests.
Cochlear implants bypass damaged hair cells delivering electrical signals directly to the auditory nerve.
Paramedics provide advanced life support: intubation, IV medications, cardiac monitoring, defibrillation.
Rehabilitation medicine coordinates recovery after stroke, spinal cord injury, and amputation.
Prosthetics replace missing limbs; orthotics support weakened or deformed limbs.
Audiologists perform pure tone audiometry, tympanometry, and otoacoustic emissions testing.
Ergonomics applies biomechanics to workspace design, reducing repetitive strain injuries.
Radiographers use the ALARA principle (as low as reasonably achievable) for radiation protection.
Dietitians use medical nutrition therapy to manage diabetes, renal disease, and malnutrition.
Patient education by allied health professionals improves medication adherence and self-management.
TRADITIONAL & ALTERNATIVE MEDICINE: TCM uses acupuncture at meridian points to regulate qi; it triggers endogenous opioid release.
Ayurveda balances three doshas (Vata, Pitta, Kapha) through diet, herbs, and lifestyle.
Systematic reviews of homeopathy find no efficacy beyond placebo despite widespread global use.
Artemisinin from Artemisia annua is a proven antimalarial validating some herbal medicine approaches.
Chiropractic spinal manipulation has evidence for acute low back pain; evidence is limited for other conditions.
Placebo effect produces measurable physiological changes via expectation and conditioning.
Mindfulness-based stress reduction reduces anxiety, depression, and chronic pain.
Traditional medicine provides primary healthcare for about 80% of populations in developing countries.
St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4, reducing efficacy of oral contraceptives - a serious drug-herb interaction.
Meditation increases alpha and theta brainwaves; cortisol levels decrease with regular practice.
The nocebo effect produces adverse outcomes from negative expectations about treatment.
Indigenous healing practices carry cultural and spiritual meaning; forced abandonment causes lasting harm.
Heavy metal contamination and adulteration are documented safety concerns in herbal products.
Integrative medicine combines evidence-based conventional care with well-studied complementary approaches.
Both nocebo and placebo effects demonstrate the clinical significance of the mind-body connection.
COMPUTER SCIENCE: Time complexity O(n log n) sorting outperforms O(n^2) for large n; big-O notation ignores constants.
Turing's halting problem: no algorithm can decide whether arbitrary programs halt (undecidability).
Gradient descent minimizes loss by updating parameters in the direction of steepest decrease.
CAP theorem: distributed systems can guarantee at most two of consistency, availability, partition tolerance.
RSA encryption relies on the difficulty of factoring large semiprimes; 2048-bit keys are current standard.
SQL B-tree indexes enable O(log n) lookups; the query optimizer selects execution plans from statistics.
TCP provides reliable byte-stream transport via sequence numbers, ACKs, and retransmission.
Neural networks approximate any function by stacking weighted sums and nonlinear activations like ReLU.
Git stores content-addressed objects; each commit hashes its tree and parent references.
P vs NP: whether polynomial-time verifiable problems are also solvable in polynomial time is unknown.
Von Neumann bottleneck: memory bandwidth limits CPU performance in classic architectures.
MapReduce processes large datasets by mapping keys across nodes then aggregating reduced values.
Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths in O((V+E)log V) using a priority queue.
Docker containers package apps with dependencies; Kubernetes orchestrates container scheduling.
L1 cache access is about 1 nanosecond vs RAM at about 100 nanoseconds; sequential access exploits spatial locality.
Blockchain is an append-only hash-chained ledger; proof-of-work makes tampering computationally costly.
Regular expressions define patterns via finite automata; DFAs recognize regular languages in O(n).
Object-oriented programming encapsulates state and behavior; polymorphism enables substitutability.
Backpropagation computes gradients via the chain rule, enabling multi-layer neural network training.
Public-key cryptography uses asymmetric key pairs; the public key encrypts, the private key decrypts.
ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING: Ohm's law V = IR; Kirchhoff's voltage law states the sum of voltages around any closed loop equals zero.
Op-amps have near-infinite input impedance; negative feedback sets stable closed-loop gain.
CMOS logic uses complementary NFET/PFET pairs, consuming power only during switching transitions.
FFT computes the Discrete Fourier Transform in O(n log n) instead of O(n^2).
PID controllers combine proportional, integral, and derivative terms for closed-loop control.
Power transmission at high voltage reduces I^2*R losses proportional to 1/V^2.
NAND gates are functionally complete: any logic circuit can be built from NAND gates alone.
Phase-locked loops lock output oscillator phase to a reference; used in clock recovery and synthesis.
p-n junctions rectify current; forward bias lowers the depletion barrier for carrier injection.
ADCs must sample at twice the Nyquist rate to prevent aliasing; bit resolution determines quantization noise.
Shannon channel capacity C = B * log2(1+SNR) sets the theoretical maximum data rate.
Antennas convert electrical signals to electromagnetic waves; gain, directivity, and bandwidth are key.
Power electronics uses IGBTs and MOSFETs to convert and control electrical power at high efficiency.
Semiconductor doping introduces n-type (donor) or p-type (acceptor) majority carriers.
Bode plots show gain and phase vs. frequency; gain margin and phase margin measure stability.
MECHANICAL ENGINEERING: Young's modulus E = stress/strain where stress = F/A and strain = delta_L/L.
Four-stroke engine: intake, compression, power (combustion), exhaust; thermal efficiency about 25 to 35%.
Carnot efficiency equals 1 - T_cold/T_hot and sets the theoretical maximum for any heat engine.
Reynolds number Re = rho*v*L/mu; laminar flow below about 2300, turbulent above about 4000 in pipes.
Bernoulli equation P + 0.5*rho*v^2 + rho*g*h = constant along a streamline.
Fatigue failure occurs below yield strength under cyclic loading; S-N curves map cycles to failure.
Finite element analysis divides structures into elements, solving stiffness matrices to predict stress.
Fourier's law governs conduction; Newton's cooling governs convection; Stefan-Boltzmann governs radiation.
Gear ratios alter torque and speed; power equals torque times angular velocity in ideal gear trains.
Vibration resonance occurs when forcing frequency matches natural frequency; damping prevents failure.
Tribology studies friction, wear, and lubrication; the Stribeck curve maps friction regimes vs. speed.
Additive manufacturing builds parts layer by layer; SLS, FDM, and SLA differ in material and resolution.
GD&T standardizes geometric tolerancing for form, orientation, location, and runout.
Hydraulic systems transmit power via incompressible fluid; Pascal's law equalizes pressure throughout.
Thermal expansion coefficient alpha = (1/L)(dL/dT) is critical in precision machinery design.
CIVIL ENGINEERING: Structural equilibrium requires sum of forces = 0 and sum of moments = 0.
Concrete compressive strength increases as water-cement ratio decreases.
Pile foundations transfer loads to competent strata when surface soils lack bearing capacity.
Manning's equation governs open channel flow; flow rate depends on area, hydraulic radius, slope, and roughness.
Level of service A through F measures traffic flow quality from free flow to breakdown.
Terzaghi's consolidation theory explains time-dependent settlement of saturated clay under load.
Prestressed concrete introduces compressive stress counteracting tensile loads, enabling longer spans.
Seismic design uses ductility and energy dissipation; base isolation decouples structures from ground motion.
Cable-stayed and suspension bridges use tension to span hundreds of meters economically.
CBR values and equivalent single axle loading determine flexible pavement layer thicknesses.
Rankine earth pressure theory gives active and passive pressure coefficients for retaining wall design.
Critical path method identifies the longest project sequence; float allows delay on non-critical tasks.
Environmental engineering designs coagulation, filtration, and disinfection for drinking water treatment.
Urban heat island effect occurs because urban surfaces absorb more solar radiation than vegetated areas.
Geosynthetics reinforce soils, separate layers, and contain contaminants in civil engineering applications.
CHEMICAL ENGINEERING: Mass and energy balances on control volumes are the fundamental tools of process design.
McCabe-Thiele method graphically determines theoretical stages for binary distillation.
CSTRs achieve uniform composition; PFRs have concentration gradients along the reactor length.
Pinch analysis minimizes utility use by recovering heat between hot and cold process streams.
Darcy-Weisbach equation calculates head loss in pipes using friction factor, length, diameter, and velocity.
Peng-Robinson equation of state models non-ideal gas and liquid behavior in process simulation.
HAZOP studies systematically identify deviations using guidewords such as No, More, Less, and Reverse.
Haber-Bosch synthesis of ammonia uses iron catalyst at 400 to 500 degrees C and 150 to 300 atm.
Scale-up requires matching dimensionless groups (Reynolds, Prandtl, Damkohler) to preserve process behavior.
Green chemistry minimizes waste, uses renewable feedstocks, and designs safer chemicals.
Membrane separation uses selective permeability without phase change; reverse osmosis desalinates water.
Fluidized beds provide uniform temperature and good gas-solid contact for catalytic cracking.
Chemical vapor deposition deposits thin films for semiconductors and optical coatings.
Biochemical engineering applies process engineering to fermentation and downstream protein purification.
Distillation composition control uses reflux ratio and reboiler duty as manipulated variables.
MATERIALS SCIENCE & ENGINEERING: FCC, BCC, and HCP crystal structures differ in packing fraction and ductility.
Dislocations are line defects enabling plastic deformation; strain hardening increases dislocation density.
The iron-carbon phase diagram governs steel metallurgy; the eutectoid point is at 0.76 wt% C and 727 degrees C.
Stress intensity factor K_I = sigma*sqrt(pi*a) describes crack-tip stress fields in fracture mechanics.
Band gaps between 0 and 3 eV define semiconductors; doping shifts the Fermi level.
Above critical molecular weight, polymer chains entangle, dramatically increasing melt viscosity.
Ceramics are brittle due to ionic and covalent bonding and low dislocation mobility.
Composites combine reinforcement fibers in a matrix achieving properties unattainable in either alone.
Galvanic corrosion occurs when dissimilar metals contact in an electrolyte.
Martensite forms when steel is rapidly quenched, trapping carbon in a body-centered tetragonal structure.
Gold nanoparticles appear red due to surface plasmon resonance, a quantum size effect.
Atomic layer deposition produces conformal nanometer-thick films via self-limiting surface reactions.
Shape-memory alloys recover their shape on heating via austenite-martensite transformation.
Scanning electron microscopy images surfaces at nanometer resolution; EDX identifies elemental composition.
Biomaterials require biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and mechanical compatibility with host tissue.
NUCLEAR ENGINEERING: Nuclear fission of uranium-235 releases about 200 MeV per event, about 50 million times more than chemical combustion.
Chain reactions sustain when k_eff = 1 (criticality); k_eff less than 1 is subcritical.
Moderators slow fast neutrons to thermal energies for higher fission cross-sections.
Control rods absorb neutrons to regulate reactor power; negative temperature coefficients add passive safety.
Alpha particles have range of centimeters in air; gamma radiation requires lead or concrete shielding.
The Gray measures absorbed dose in J/kg; the Sievert is effective dose accounting for radiation type.
Fusion of deuterium and tritium releases 17.6 MeV but requires temperature above 100 million K.
High-level radioactive waste contains fission products and actinides; heat generation declines over centuries.
Pressurized water reactors use primary and secondary loops to prevent radioactive steam in turbines.
Half-lives: uranium-238 is 4.5 billion years; cesium-137 is 30 years; iodine-131 is 8 days.
Monte Carlo transport simulations model probabilistic neutron interactions in complex geometries.
Technetium-99m is the most used medical radioisotope for SPECT imaging due to its 6-hour half-life.
Spent nuclear fuel is more than 95% uranium-238, about 1% plutonium-239, and about 4% fission products.
Cherenkov radiation produces blue glow when charged particles exceed light's phase velocity in water.
The Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty distinguishes nuclear-weapon states from non-nuclear-weapon states.
METAPHYSICS: Metaphysics investigates existence, identity, space, time, causality, and the nature of possibility.
The mind-body problem: dualism posits two substances; physicalism holds mental states are physical.
Modal realism (Lewis): all possible worlds exist as concrete, spatiotemporally isolated universes.
The hard problem of consciousness: explaining why physical processes produce subjective experience.
Free will debate: compatibilism holds free will is compatible with determinism; hard incompatibilism denies this.
Causation theories include Humean regularity, counterfactual, and mechanistic accounts.
Problem of universals: realism holds properties exist independently; nominalism holds they are concepts.
Eternalism holds past, present, and future equally exist; presentism holds only the present is real.
Supervenience: there is no mental difference without physical difference.
Abstract objects like numbers exist outside space-time in Platonist ontologies.
Process philosophy (Whitehead): becoming rather than being is fundamental; reality consists of events.
Principle of sufficient reason (Leibniz): everything has a reason or cause for its existence.
Personal identity accounts include psychological continuity, physical continuity, and narrative identity.
The simulation argument (Bostrom): posthuman civilizations could run vast numbers of ancestor simulations.
Ontological categories include substance, property, relation, event, process, and abstract object.
EPISTEMOLOGY: Plato's justified true belief account of knowledge was refuted by Gettier's 1963 counterexamples.
Foundationalism holds all beliefs rest on non-inferential basic beliefs immune to infinite regress.
Coherentism holds beliefs gain warrant from mutual support within a coherent network.
A priori knowledge is justified independently of experience; a posteriori knowledge requires experience.
Bayesian epistemology models degrees of belief as probabilities updated via Bayes' theorem.
Reliabilism holds a belief is justified if formed by a reliable cognitive process.
Hume's problem of induction: past regularities give no logical guarantee of future patterns.
Epistemic virtues including open-mindedness and intellectual humility promote knowledge acquisition.
Social epistemology examines how peer disagreement may require belief revision.
The regress problem is addressed differently by foundationalism, coherentism, and infinitism.
Pragmatism holds truth is what works in practice; beliefs are tools for navigating experience.
Phenomenology uses bracketing (epoche) to suspend factual assumptions and study pure experience.
Kuhn's paradigm shifts: anomaly accumulation triggers revolutionary science replacing old paradigms.
Testimony transmits knowledge; reductionists require corroboration while anti-reductionists allow default trust.
Externalism holds justification depends on factors external to the agent's own perspective.
ETHICS: Utilitarianism maximizes aggregate well-being; act utilitarianism evaluates individual acts by consequences.
Kant's categorical imperative: act only on universalizable maxims; treat persons as ends, never merely means.
Virtue ethics (Aristotle) focuses on character traits enabling human flourishing (eudaimonia).
Moral relativism holds moral truths are culture-relative; moral realism holds moral facts are objective.
The trolley problem tests intuitions about act versus omission and direct versus redirected harm.
Rawls's veil of ignorance: principles chosen without knowing one's position define just institutions.
Rights-based ethics holds individuals have inviolable rights constraining aggregate-benefit trade-offs.
Moral psychology dual-process theory distinguishes fast intuitive from slow deliberative moral judgment.
Effective altruism applies evidence and reason to maximize welfare per unit of resources.
Singer's animal ethics: sentience grounds moral consideration; suffering matters regardless of species.
Meta-ethics asks whether moral claims are truth-apt and what makes them true.
Care ethics emphasizes relational responsibilities and particular others over universal principles.
Contractarianism grounds ethics in what rational agents would agree to under fair conditions.
Moral progress is evidenced by abolition of slavery and expansion of civil rights.
Existentialist ethics (Sartre): radical freedom entails radical responsibility for authentic choice.
LOGIC: Modus ponens: from P and P implies Q, infer Q. Modus tollens: from not-Q and P implies Q, infer not-P.
Tautologies are true under all truth valuations; contradictions are false under all valuations.
First-order logic adds universal and existential quantifiers; Godel's completeness theorem shows it is sound and complete.
Kripke semantics models modal logic with possible worlds and accessibility relations.
Proof by contradiction: assume not-P, derive a contradiction, then conclude P.
De Morgan's laws: not(A and B) = not-A or not-B; not(A or B) = not-A and not-B.
Soundness means every provable sentence is valid; completeness means every valid sentence is provable.
The law of excluded middle (P or not-P) is rejected by intuitionistic logic, which requires constructive proofs.
Ad hominem attacks the person rather than the argument; straw man misrepresents the position.
Fuzzy logic assigns truth values in [0,1]; used in control systems and natural language processing.
Syllogistic logic determines validity from categorical statement types and arrangement.
Relevance logic requires premises to be relevantly connected to conclusions.
Probability theory extends logic to degrees of belief using Kolmogorov's axioms.
Mathematical induction proves claims for all natural numbers via base case and inductive step.
Argument mapping visualizes premise-conclusion structure and identifies support, objection, and rebuttal.
POLITICAL PHILOSOPHY: Social contract theory grounds political authority in rational consent to escape the state of nature.
Rawlsian liberalism: the difference principle allows inequalities only if they benefit the least advantaged.
Communitarianism critiques liberal atomism; identity and values are community-constituted.
Marxism holds class conflict and historical materialism determine social and political structures.
Republicanism emphasizes non-domination: freedom requires absence of arbitrary power over one.
Weber distinguished traditional, charismatic, and legal-rational types of legitimate authority.
Global justice debate: cosmopolitanism extends justice to all humans; nationalism prioritizes co-citizens.
Anarchism rejects all coercive authority as illegitimate.
The capability approach (Sen, Nussbaum) holds institutions should enable human capabilities to flourish.
Montesquieu's separation of powers divides government to prevent tyranny.
Civil disobedience (Rawls, King): public, non-violent law-breaking targeted at unjust laws.
Federalism: subsidiarity assigns power to the lowest competent level of government.
Theories of punishment include retributivism, deterrence, rehabilitation, and incapacitation.
Democracy's justifications include preference aggregation, deliberative legitimacy, and equal standing.
Multiculturalism advocates public recognition of minority cultural identities within pluralistic states.
PHILOSOPHY OF SCIENCE: Falsificationism (Popper): scientific theories must make predictions that could in principle be refuted.
Duhem-Quine thesis: individual hypotheses cannot be tested in isolation from auxiliary assumptions.
Kuhn's paradigms: normal science refines them; crises and anomaly accumulation trigger revolutions.
Scientific realism holds successful theories approximately describe reality; anti-realism focuses on empirical adequacy.
Inference to the best explanation selects the hypothesis that best explains the available evidence.
Underdetermination: for any finite evidence, infinitely many theories are logically compatible with it.
The demarcation problem: no single criterion cleanly separates science from pseudoscience.
Bayesian confirmation: evidence E confirms hypothesis H if P(H|E) > P(H).
Scientific models are idealized representations; accuracy-simplicity trade-offs are pragmatically shaped.
Value-ladenness: theory choice involves epistemic virtues like simplicity that are themselves value-laden.
Laws of nature can be interpreted as Humean regularities or as genuine necessities.
Controlled experiments isolate variables; randomization and blinding reduce confounds.
Social factors including funding and institutional culture influence which research questions are asked.
Quantum mechanics raises unresolved interpretation issues: Copenhagen, many-worlds, and pilot wave.
Reductionism holds chemistry reduces to physics; emergence suggests some higher-level properties may be irreducible.
AESTHETICS: Kant held judgments of taste claim universal agreement yet are grounded in subjective feeling.
Art as expression theory holds art externalizes inner states; appreciation involves empathetic reconstruction.
Formalism evaluates art by formal properties like line, color, and composition.
Institutional theory of art (Dickie): arthood is conferred by the artworld as a social institution.
The sublime involves overwhelming power producing awe mixed with rational self-affirmation.
Mimesis (Aristotle): representation of reality as an aesthetic ideal.
Aesthetic experience involves heightened perceptual attention; Dewey argued it consummated ordinary experience.
The paradox of fiction: audiences respond emotionally to characters they know are non-existent.
Narrative theory analyzes plot, character, time, focalization, and the story/discourse distinction.
Musical aesthetics debates whether music represents or expresses emotions or is purely formal.
Cultural aesthetics recognizes that standards of beauty vary across cultures and historical periods.
Performance art collapses the art-object and art-process distinction, foregrounding presence.
Aesthetic value may diverge from moral value; beautiful things can depict evil.
Digital art and AI generation challenge authorship and creativity concepts.
Photography's advent shifted painting toward impression and abstraction.
MAJOR PHILOSOPHICAL TRADITIONS: Continental rationalism (Descartes, Spinoza, Leibniz) derives knowledge from innate ideas via reason.
British empiricism (Locke, Berkeley, Hume) holds all knowledge derives from sensory experience.
Kant synthesized rationalism and empiricism: the mind actively structures experience via categories.
Pragmatism (Peirce, James, Dewey) evaluates ideas by their practical consequences.
Phenomenology (Husserl, Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty) describes conscious experience through first-person analysis.
Existentialism (Sartre, Camus) emphasizes radical freedom and authenticity in confrontation with absurdity.
Analytic philosophy uses logical analysis of language; many philosophical problems are conceptual confusions.
Structuralism (Saussure) holds meaning arises from differences within systems.
Post-structuralism (Derrida, Foucault) deconstructs binaries and analyzes power-knowledge relations.
Confucianism grounds social harmony in five relationships governed by ren (benevolence) and li (propriety).
Taoism teaches alignment with the Tao via wu wei (effortless action) and simplicity.
Buddhist philosophy addresses impermanence, no-self, and dependent origination.
African philosophy Ubuntu holds personhood is relational: 'I am because we are.'
Frankfurt School critical theory critiques ideology, instrumental reason, and the culture industry.
Feminist philosophy critiques androcentrism; standpoint epistemology argues perspective is epistemically significant.
ABRAHAMIC RELIGIONS: Torah (five books of Moses) forms the core of Jewish scripture; Talmud codifies rabbinical interpretation.
Christianity centers on Jesus as the Messiah; the New Testament records his teachings.
Islam's five pillars include shahada, salat (five daily prayers), zakat, fasting, and hajj.
The doctrine of the Trinity was formalized at the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE.
Islamic jurisprudence derives law from Quran, Hadith, consensus (ijma), and analogical reasoning (qiyas).
The Protestant Reformation (1517) challenged papal authority, emphasizing Scripture and faith alone.
Sufism seeks direct experience of the divine through love and devotion; Rumi exemplifies this.
Dead Sea Scrolls discovered in 1947 predate previous Hebrew manuscripts by 1000 years.
Liberation theology links Christian ethics to struggle for social and economic justice.
Kabbalah maps divine emanations via the Sefirot (Tree of Life); Ein Sof is the infinite divine.
Ecumenism seeks unity among Christian denominations; interfaith dialogue promotes understanding.
Biblical textual criticism analyzes manuscript transmission to reconstruct original texts.
Abrahamic eschatologies include Jewish messianism, Christian second coming, and Islamic Day of Judgment.
Mystical traditions in each Abrahamic faith emphasize direct experience over doctrine.
The Crusades (1095 to 1291) were religiously motivated military campaigns with lasting consequences.
DHARMIC RELIGIONS: Vedas, Upanishads, Bhagavad Gita, and Puranas form major strata of Hindu scriptural tradition.
Advaita Vedanta: Brahman (ultimate reality) and Atman (individual self) are ultimately identical.
Buddhism's Four Noble Truths: suffering, craving, cessation, and the Eightfold Path to liberation.
Jainism holds ahimsa (non-violence) is the supreme ethical principle; all living beings have souls.
Sikhism (Guru Nanak, 1469) teaches one God, equality, service (seva), and rejection of caste.
Karma holds intentional actions create tendencies affecting future experiences across lives.
Theravada Buddhism emphasizes the Pali Canon and individual liberation; Mahayana adds the bodhisattva ideal.
Yoga paths include Jnana (knowledge), Bhakti (devotion), Karma (action), and Raja (meditation).
Hindu temples are designed as cosmic mandalas; puja involves offerings, hymns, and circumambulation.
Jain syadvada logic holds all judgments are conditional and qualified by 'in some respect.'
The Mahabharata (including the Bhagavad Gita) and Ramayana are central epic texts of Hindu culture.
The Buddhist Sangha preserves Dharma; Vinaya texts govern monastic discipline.
Vipassana meditation cultivates insight into impermanence, suffering, and no-self.
B.R. Ambedkar led reform against caste discrimination and led a mass conversion to Buddhism.
Dharma as cosmic order, righteousness, and duty is central to both Hindu and Buddhist ethics.
EAST ASIAN RELIGIONS: Confucianism's five relationships are governed by ren (benevolence) and li (ritual propriety).
Taoism teaches that wu wei (non-action) aligns one with the Tao; Zhuangzi used paradox to convey this.
Zen (Chan) Buddhism emphasizes direct experience, koans, and zazen meditation over doctrinal study.
Shinto kami inhabit natural phenomena; shrines (jinja) are sacred spaces for ritual.
Neo-Confucianism synthesized Confucian ethics with Buddhist and Taoist metaphysics in the Song dynasty.
The Tao Te Ching's 81 chapters emphasize paradox, simplicity, and alignment with natural processes.
Analects of Confucius: virtue (ren), ritual propriety (li), and exemplary personhood (junzi).
Pure Land Buddhism: reciting Amitabha's name (nembutsu) enables rebirth in the pure land.
The Tripitaka Koreana consists of 81258 woodblocks of Buddhist scripture produced in Goryeo Korea.
The I Ching organizes divination around 64 hexagrams and influenced Chinese cosmology.
Imperial civil service exams (keju) selected officials based on Confucian classics.
The concept of li (principle/pattern) in Neo-Confucianism describes rational structure in all phenomena.
Feng shui aligns spatial arrangements with qi flow, influencing architecture and site selection.
East Asian religions share emphasis on harmony, ancestors, and this-worldly ethical cultivation.
Korean Seon and Japanese Zen emphasize direct mind-to-mind transmission beyond words.
INDIGENOUS & TRIBAL RELIGIONS: Animism attributes spiritual significance to natural entities: animals, plants, rivers, and mountains.
Shamanism involves ritual specialists entering altered states to communicate with spirits for healing.
Oral traditions preserve cosmologies, moral codes, and historical knowledge across generations.
Aboriginal Australian Dreamtime describes ancestral beings who created the landscape through mythic events.
Native American traditions vary widely; common themes include sacred circles, vision quests, and reverence for nature.
African traditional religions emphasize ancestor veneration, divination, and community healing rituals.
Sacred geography treats mountains, rivers, and forests as spiritually charged sites of ritual practice.
Syncretism: Candomble merges Yoruba orishas with Catholic saints in Brazilian religious practice.
Mana (Polynesia) is spiritual power; tapu (taboo) protects sacred objects and persons.
Potlatch ceremonies in the Pacific Northwest redistribute wealth and reinforce social relations.
Vision quests among Plains tribes involve solitary fasting to receive spiritual guidance.
Indigenous ecological knowledge encodes sustainable land management developed over millennia.
Totemism links social groups to sacred animals or objects symbolizing group identity and ancestry.
Loss of indigenous religious practice through colonialism caused deep cultural trauma.
Revitalization movements seek to restore indigenous traditions and assert cultural sovereignty.
ARTS & CREATIVE EXPRESSION: Renaissance perspective (Brunelleschi) and chiaroscuro transformed painting in 14th to 17th century Europe.
Cubism (Picasso, Braque) fragmented perspective into simultaneous viewpoints on a flat surface.
Abstract Expressionism (Pollock, Rothko) prioritized emotional intensity and non-representation.
Counterpoint (Bach) layers independent melodic lines; the fugue develops a subject through episodes.
Film montage (Eisenstein): juxtaposition of shots creates meaning beyond individual images.
Brecht's alienation effect (Verfremdungseffekt) prevents audience identification to enable critical thinking.
Impressionism captured light and moment rather than precise detail; this freed painting from representation.
International Style architecture: 'less is more' (Mies van der Rohe); form follows function.
Jazz bebop (Parker, Gillespie) emphasized improvisation and complex harmony over dance rhythms.
Stream-of-consciousness technique (Woolf, Joyce) renders interior experience through unfiltered thought flow.
Postcolonial literature (Achebe, Morrison, Rushdie) reclaims indigenous voices and critiques colonialism.
Surrealism embraced the unconscious, chance, and dream imagery to challenge rational aesthetics.
Typography uses visual hierarchy, white space, and typeface selection to communicate and guide attention.
Dance encodes cultural knowledge; ballet formalized court dance while Isadora Duncan rejected its constraints.
Avant-garde movements like Dada challenged conventional aesthetics by embracing irrationality.
Chiaroscuro is the painting technique of using strong contrasts between light and dark to model three-dimensional form and create drama, mastered by Caravaggio and Rembrandt.
Sonata form has three sections: exposition (introducing two contrasting themes), development (transforming them through different keys), and recapitulation (restating them resolved in the home key).
Linear perspective, formalized by Brunelleschi in the Renaissance, creates depth on a flat surface by converging parallel lines toward a vanishing point.
The color wheel organizes hues; complementary colors sit opposite each other and create vibrant contrast, while analogous colors sit adjacent and harmonize.
Impressionism (Monet, Renoir) captured fleeting light and atmosphere with loose, visible brushstrokes, breaking from precise academic realism in the 1870s.
A musical scale divides the octave into steps; the major scale follows the pattern whole-whole-half-whole-whole-whole-half and sounds bright, while the minor sounds darker.
Counterpoint is the art of combining independent melodic lines into a coherent texture, perfected by J.S. Bach in works like the fugues.
The golden ratio (about 1.618) appears in art and architecture as a proportion many find aesthetically pleasing, from the Parthenon to Renaissance compositions.
Cubism (Picasso, Braque) fractured objects into geometric planes and showed multiple viewpoints simultaneously, rejecting single-point perspective around 1907.
A narrative's three-act structure moves through setup, confrontation, and resolution; the protagonist faces rising conflict toward a climax and denouement.
Harmony is the simultaneous sounding of notes; a major chord stacks a root, major third, and perfect fifth, while a minor chord lowers the third.
Photography exposure depends on the triangle of aperture (depth of field), shutter speed (motion), and ISO (sensitivity to light).
Sculpture techniques include carving (subtractive, from stone or wood), modeling (additive, with clay), and casting (pouring material into a mold).
Greek tragedy followed Aristotle's Poetics, using a tragic hero whose fatal flaw (hamartia) leads to a reversal of fortune and catharsis in the audience.
HUMANITIES: Historiography analyzes how historical narratives are constructed from evidence and perspective.
Saussure distinguished langue (the language system) from parole (individual speech acts).
Archaeological stratigraphy establishes chronological sequences; radiocarbon dating provides absolute dates.
Comparative religion identifies cross-cultural patterns; Campbell's monomyth (hero's journey) recurs globally.
Cultural studies (Stuart Hall) analyzes how culture produces and reproduces power relations.
Gadamer's hermeneutics: understanding requires a fusion of horizons between text and interpreter.
Geertz's thick description captures cultural meaning from the perspective of social actors.
Gender studies examines how gender is socially constructed and intersects with race, class, and power.
Classical rhetoric (Aristotle): ethos (credibility), pathos (emotion), logos (logic) as persuasion modes.
Benjamin's translation theory: translations transform originals and give them an afterlife.
Digital humanities applies computational methods including text mining, network analysis, and GIS mapping.
Oral history records individual testimonies; memory studies examines selective preservation.
Medieval material culture (manuscripts, artifacts) reveals non-teleological social and intellectual diversity.
Homer's Iliad and Odyssey are foundational texts of Western literary tradition.
Translation theory: fidelity vs. fluency reflects tension between source and target language norms.
SOCIAL SCIENCES: Durkheim distinguished mechanical solidarity (small-scale, shared values) from organic solidarity (complex division of labor).
Weber's Protestant Ethic held Calvinist asceticism created cultural preconditions for capitalism.
Random sampling enables generalization; response bias and question wording threaten survey validity.
Bourdieu held cultural and social capital supplement economic capital as stratification resources.
Labeling theory (Becker): applying deviant labels can amplify rule-breaking behavior.
Rational choice theory models political actors as utility-maximizers.
IR realism emphasizes power and self-interest; liberalism emphasizes cooperation and institutions.
Embeddedness (Granovetter): markets are social structures, not autonomous rational systems.
The sociological imagination (C. Wright Mills) connects personal troubles to public issues.
Social movement success depends on resources, political opportunity, and strategic framing.
Simmel: city life produces mental reserve and blase attitudes as adaptations to overstimulation.
Network analysis: weak ties (Granovetter) bridge clusters and transmit novel information.
Ethnographic participant observation with thick description captures meaning from actors' perspectives.
Institutional isomorphism: organizations conform to normative pressures, producing structural similarity.
Intersectionality (Crenshaw): overlapping identities create compounded experiences of oppression.
The bystander effect is the finding that the more people present at an emergency, the less likely any individual is to help, due to diffusion of responsibility.
Cultural relativism is the principle that a culture's beliefs and practices should be understood on their own terms rather than judged against another culture's standards.
Conformity experiments by Asch showed people often agree with an obviously wrong group answer, while Milgram's obedience studies showed willingness to harm under authority.
Socialization is the lifelong process by which people internalize their society's norms, values, and roles through family, school, peers, and media.
Max Weber distinguished three types of legitimate authority: traditional (custom), charismatic (personal magnetism), and legal-rational (rules and offices).
Anthropology studies humanity holistically across cultures and time, using ethnographic fieldwork and participant observation to understand social practices.
Demographic transition theory describes how societies move from high birth and death rates to low ones as they industrialize, driving population growth then stabilization.
Social stratification ranks people in hierarchies of class, status, and power; Marx framed it as class conflict between owners and workers over the means of production.
In-group/out-group bias leads people to favor those they identify with and discriminate against outsiders, a root of prejudice and intergroup conflict.
Symbolic interactionism holds that society emerges from the meanings people create and exchange through everyday interaction and shared symbols.
Globalization integrates economies, cultures, and populations through trade, technology, and migration, producing both interdependence and cultural friction.
Survey research and controlled experiments are core social-science methods; correlation does not imply causation, and confounding variables must be controlled.
LAW & JURISPRUDENCE: Common law derives rules from judicial precedent (stare decisis); civil law uses comprehensive codes.
Marbury v. Madison (1803) established judicial review of legislation in the US constitutional system.
Criminal law requires proof beyond reasonable doubt; guilty act plus guilty mind are usually required.
Negligence requires duty, breach, causation (but-for test), and quantifiable damage.
Contract law enforces voluntary agreements; consideration, offer, and acceptance are foundational.
UN Charter Article 2(4) prohibits force; Article 51 permits self-defense.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) articulates universal civil, political, and social rights.
Legal positivism (Hart) holds law is a social fact; natural law theory requires moral minimum standards.
Hearsay is generally inadmissible; exceptions allow reliable out-of-court statements as evidence.
The rule of law requires laws to be public, prospective, stable, and applied equally.
The Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable searches; Miranda protects Fifth Amendment rights.
Restorative justice focuses on repairing harm and restoring relationships rather than punishment.
Comparative law studies similarities across legal systems; legal transplants adapt foreign rules.
Administrative due process requires notice and opportunity to be heard before adverse government action.
The International Criminal Court prosecutes genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity.
Habeas corpus is a legal right requiring authorities to bring a detained person before a court to justify the detention, protecting against unlawful imprisonment.

Civil law resolves disputes between private parties (contracts, property, torts) with the burden of 'preponderance of the evidence,' while criminal law prosecutes offenses against the state requiring proof 'beyond a reasonable doubt.'
The presumption of innocence places the burden on the prosecution to prove guilt; the accused need not prove innocence.
Common law systems (UK, US) rely on judicial precedent (stare decisis), while civil law systems (France, Germany) rely on comprehensive written codes.
A contract requires offer, acceptance, consideration (something of value exchanged), and mutual intent to be legally bound.
Tort law lets injured parties seek compensation for harm caused by another's negligence, intentional acts, or strict liability.
Due process guarantees fair legal procedures before the state deprives someone of life, liberty, or property, enshrined in the US Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments.
Intellectual property law protects creations: patents cover inventions, copyrights cover creative works, trademarks cover brands, and trade secrets cover confidential business information.
Mens rea (guilty mind) and actus reus (guilty act) are both generally required to establish criminal liability.
International law governs relations between states through treaties, customary law, and bodies like the International Court of Justice and the UN.
Judicial review allows courts to strike down laws that conflict with the constitution, established in the US by Marbury v. Madison (1803).
The exclusionary rule bars evidence obtained through illegal searches, deterring police misconduct under the Fourth Amendment.
ECONOMICS & BUSINESS: Markets clear when quantity supplied equals quantity demanded; shifts change the equilibrium.
Comparative advantage (Ricardo): mutual gains from trade arise from specializing in lower-opportunity-cost goods.
Monetary policy uses interest rates and money supply to stabilize inflation and employment.
Efficient market hypothesis: asset prices reflect all available information.
Nash equilibrium: no player benefits by unilaterally changing strategy given others' strategies.
Behavioral economics: loss aversion, availability heuristic, and anchoring bias depart from rationality.
Discounted cash flow analysis values investments; WACC is the hurdle rate for capital allocation.
Adverse selection and moral hazard are core market failures from information asymmetry.
Porter's Five Forces analyze industry competitiveness: rivalry, substitutes, entrants, buyer power, supplier power.
Keynesian economics: fiscal policy can stabilize aggregate demand in the short run.
Antitrust law prevents anti-competitive mergers and predatory pricing.
Returns to education: each additional year raises wages about 8 to 13%.
Gini coefficient: 0 = perfect equality, 1 = maximum inequality.
Network effects: product value increases with users; creates winner-take-all platform dynamics.
GDP = C + I + G + net exports; growth, inflation, and unemployment are macro targets.
Comparative advantage (Ricardo) shows that two parties both gain from trade when each specializes in what it produces at lower opportunity cost, even if one is better at everything.
Quantitative easing is a central-bank policy of buying long-term securities to inject money into the economy, lower interest rates, and encourage lending when rates are already near zero.
Supply and demand set market prices: price rises when demand exceeds supply and falls when supply exceeds demand, settling at the equilibrium where they cross.
Inflation is a sustained rise in the general price level, eroding purchasing power; central banks target around 2% and raise interest rates to cool it.
GDP measures the total value of goods and services produced in an economy; real GDP adjusts for inflation to compare across years.
Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best alternative forgone when making a choice; it underlies all economic decision-making.
A recession is typically defined as two consecutive quarters of declining GDP, marked by rising unemployment and falling output.
Fiscal policy uses government spending and taxation to influence the economy, while monetary policy uses interest rates and money supply controlled by the central bank.
Market failures like externalities, public goods, and monopolies justify government intervention; a carbon tax internalizes the external cost of pollution.
Compound interest grows wealth exponentially because interest earns interest; the rule of 72 estimates doubling time by dividing 72 by the annual percentage rate.
Diversification reduces investment risk by spreading money across uncorrelated assets, since not all will fall at once.
Elasticity measures how much quantity demanded responds to price changes; necessities are inelastic while luxuries are elastic.
Game theory analyzes strategic decisions; the prisoner's dilemma shows how rational individuals can fail to cooperate even when cooperation benefits both.
Marginal analysis guides decisions by comparing the additional benefit of one more unit against its additional cost; optimal output is where marginal cost equals marginal revenue.
EDUCATION: Constructivism (Piaget, Vygotsky): learners actively build knowledge; ZPD guides scaffolded instruction.
Bloom's taxonomy: recall, comprehension, application, analysis, synthesis, evaluation.
Formative assessment provides real-time feedback; summative assessment evaluates learning at end.
Spaced repetition and retrieval practice significantly improve long-term retention.
Teacher quality is the strongest in-school predictor of student achievement.
Universal Design for Learning advocates multiple means of representation, engagement, and expression.
Dewey: learning by doing, real-context problem solving, and education for democratic citizenship.
The achievement gap reflects socioeconomic, racial, and linguistic disparities in student outcomes.
Higher education massification: tertiary enrollment grew from about 10% in 1970 to about 40% globally.
Standardized testing (PISA, NAEP) provides comparable outcome measures; teaching to the test is a concern.
Early childhood education: secure attachment and stimulating environments matter most in sensitive periods.
Individualized education plans (IEPs) customize goals, services, and accommodations for students with disabilities.
Culturally responsive pedagogy adapts instruction to students' cultural backgrounds.
Online learning grew rapidly post-COVID; hybrid models may preserve benefits of each modality.
Growth mindset (Dweck): belief that abilities develop through effort predicts academic resilience.
MILITARY & WARFARE: Clausewitz: war is the continuation of politics by other means; friction and fog impede execution.
Combined arms integrates infantry, armor, artillery, air, and logistics into mutually reinforcing operations.
Mao's three-phase insurgency: strategic defense to equilibrium to strategic offense.
Nuclear deterrence rests on mutual assured destruction; second-strike capability is essential.
Geneva Conventions protect non-combatants, wounded, and prisoners; distinction and proportionality are core.
Napoleon's logistics collapsed in Russia in 1812; supply chain failure has decided many campaigns.
Cyber warfare targets critical infrastructure; attribution is technically and legally difficult.
Intelligence (HUMINT, SIGINT, IMINT) drives operational planning; Pearl Harbor was an intelligence failure.
Counterinsurgency doctrine (Galula, FM 3-24) emphasizes population-centric strategy and legitimacy.
Air power theory (Douhet, Mitchell): strategic bombing of industry and morale could be war-winning.
Precision weapons, ISR, and networked forces defined the Revolution in Military Affairs.
Arms control: START limits strategic warheads; verification uses satellite imagery and inspections.
PTSD affects 15 to 20% of combat veterans; widespread recognition only came after the Vietnam War.
Drone warfare enables remote precision strikes; legal and ethical debates concern sovereignty and accountability.
Carrier strike groups provide global power projection; submarines provide second-strike nuclear capability.
AGRICULTURE & FOOD SYSTEMS: Green Revolution (1960s to 1970s): high-yield varieties, irrigation, and fertilizers tripled grain production in Asia.
Soil health depends on organic matter, microbial communities, and structure; no-till farming reduces erosion.
Irrigation accounts for about 70% of global freshwater use; drip irrigation reduces consumption by 30 to 50%.
C4 plants like maize and sorghum are more photosynthetically efficient than C3 plants in high temperature conditions.
Crop rotation breaks pest cycles; nitrogen-fixing legumes replenish soil nitrogen.
Integrated pest management combines biological, cultural, and chemical controls.
Food security requires availability, access, utilization, and stability of nutritious food.
Food loss and waste amount to about one-third of global food production annually.
Animal agriculture contributes about 14.5% of global greenhouse gas emissions.
Aquaculture provides about 50% of fish consumed globally.
Agroforestry integrates trees with crops or livestock, improving biodiversity, soil, and resilience.
GMO crops can enhance yield, pest resistance, and nutrition; regulation varies globally.
Fermentation transforms raw ingredients via microbial action; it is among the oldest preservation methods.
Fair trade aims to ensure ethical prices and labor conditions for smallholder farmers.
Urban agriculture including vertical farms improves food access and reduces transport emissions.
ENVIRONMENTAL SCIENCE & SUSTAINABILITY: Planetary boundaries framework identifies nine Earth system processes; transgression risks destabilizing the Holocene.
Species extinction rates are 100 to 1000 times background; habitat loss is the primary driver.
Oceans and vegetation absorb about 50% of anthropogenic CO2; the remainder accumulates in the atmosphere.
Ice-albedo and permafrost methane feedbacks amplify warming beyond direct CO2 forcing.
The Paris Agreement (2015) targets 1.5 to 2 degrees C warming limit; current pledges track 2.5 to 3 degrees C.
Ecosystem services including pollination, water purification, and flood control underpin enormous economic value.
Life cycle assessment quantifies environmental impacts from raw material to disposal.
Solar PV and wind costs declined 90% and 70% respectively in the 2010s, crossing grid parity.
Ocean acidification: CO2 absorption lowers ocean pH, threatening shellfish and coral.
Agricultural nitrogen runoff causes eutrophication and dead zones in coastal waters.
Tropical forests store about 45% of terrestrial carbon; deforestation releases carbon and reduces biodiversity.
Circular economy replaces take-make-waste with closed material loops of reuse, repair, and recycling.
Environmental justice: pollution burdens fall disproportionately on low-income and minority communities.
Rewilding: wolf reintroduction in Yellowstone triggered trophic cascades, restructuring the ecosystem.
Nitrogen cycle disruption from synthetic fertilizers is one of the most transgressed planetary boundaries.
COMMUNICATION & MEDIA: Shannon-Weaver model: information source, encoder, channel (with noise), decoder, destination.
Agenda-setting theory: media determines what audiences think about, not what they think.
Social media algorithms optimize engagement and empirically correlate with outrage-inducing content.
The printing press (~1450) democratized literacy and enabled the Protestant Reformation.
Journalism norms: accuracy, fairness, independence, accountability; inverted pyramid puts key facts first.
Propaganda uses selective framing, emotional appeals, and repetition to shape beliefs at scale.
Six corporations control about 90% of US media; concentration raises gatekeeping concerns.
Narrative framing shapes policy support: 'war on drugs' vs. 'public health crisis' produce different responses.
About 37% of the world lacks internet access; the digital divide tracks income, geography, age, and gender.
Advertising revenue incentivizes attention capture over content quality.
Semiotics (Saussure, Peirce): signs consist of signifier and signified; meaning is convention-dependent.
Deepfakes challenge epistemic trust; detection tools and content provenance systems are in development.
Network effects on platforms create lock-in; switching costs affect competition and consumer choice.
High-context cultures rely on implicit communication; low-context cultures favor explicit messaging.
Public relations crisis communication requires speed, transparency, and accountability.
The Shannon-Weaver model frames communication as a source encoding a message, sending it through a channel subject to noise, to a receiver who decodes it.
The spiral of silence theory (Noelle-Neumann) holds that people stay quiet about views they believe are unpopular, making the dominant opinion appear stronger than it is.
Agenda-setting theory says the media does not tell people what to think but is highly effective at telling them what to think about.
Marshall McLuhan's phrase 'the medium is the message' argues that the form of a medium shapes society more than the content it carries.
Framing shapes interpretation by emphasizing certain aspects of an issue; the same fact can read very differently depending on how it is presented.
Propaganda uses emotional appeals, repetition, and selective information to shape attitudes, distinguished from journalism by its intent to persuade rather than inform.
The two-step flow model shows that media influence often passes through opinion leaders who interpret and relay messages to their networks.
Confirmation bias and algorithmic filtering create echo chambers and filter bubbles, where people mostly encounter views that reinforce their own.
Nonverbal communication, including body language, tone, and facial expression, often conveys more emotional meaning than the words spoken.
Cultivation theory argues that heavy television viewing gradually shapes viewers' perception of reality to match the world depicted on screen.
Deepfakes use AI to fabricate realistic video and audio, eroding trust in recorded evidence and raising new challenges for verifying information.
SPORTS & PHYSICAL CULTURE: VO2max is the primary indicator of aerobic fitness; HIIT produces similar gains in less time than steady-state.
Olympic Games began in ancient Greece (~776 BCE); revived by Pierre de Coubertin in 1896.
Force plates, motion capture, and EMG are standard biomechanical analysis tools.
Periodization cycles intensity and volume to peak performance at competition time.
WADA governs global anti-doping; EPO boosts red blood cell production and endurance performance.
Sports psychology: visualization, self-talk, and pre-performance routines improve competitive performance.
Title IX (1972) mandated equal opportunity in federally funded sports; female participation tripled.
Football (soccer) is the world's most popular sport; FIFA World Cup is the most-watched single event.
Repeated head trauma is linked to CTE; NFL rule changes and helmet standards followed the research.
Random vs. blocked practice: random practice improves retention and transfer despite lower session performance.
Youth sports early specialization increases overuse injury and burnout; multi-sport participation is recommended.
E-sports drew 500+ million global viewers in 2023; prize pools rival traditional professional sports.
Jackie Robinson integrated MLB in 1947; sports has both reflected and challenged racial segregation.
Carbohydrate loading increases glycogen stores; protein timing post-exercise optimizes muscle protein synthesis.
Sports economics: media rights contracts now dwarf gate receipts in major professional leagues.
VO2 max is the maximum rate at which the body can use oxygen during intense exercise; it is the leading measure of aerobic fitness and improves with endurance training.
The offside rule in soccer penalizes an attacker who is nearer the opponent's goal line than both the ball and the second-to-last defender when the ball is played to them.
Muscle hypertrophy is the growth of muscle fibers from progressive resistance training, driven by mechanical tension, metabolic stress, and adequate protein and recovery.
Aerobic exercise uses oxygen to sustain prolonged activity, while anaerobic exercise relies on stored energy for short, intense bursts and produces lactate.
High-intensity interval training (HIIT) alternates hard efforts with recovery, improving cardiovascular fitness efficiently in less time than steady-state cardio.
Periodization structures training into cycles of varying intensity and volume to peak performance and avoid overtraining.
The body fuels exercise through three systems: the phosphocreatine system for seconds, glycolysis for minutes, and aerobic metabolism for sustained effort.
Delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS) appears 24-72 hours after unfamiliar exercise from microscopic muscle damage and subsequent repair.
Proper hydration and electrolyte balance are critical for performance; dehydration of even 2% of body weight measurably reduces endurance.
Biomechanics analyzes movement to improve technique and reduce injury, studying forces, levers, and joint angles in athletic motion.
GOVERNANCE & POLITICAL SYSTEMS: Democracy requires free elections, civil liberties, and rule of law.
Checks and balances ensure no single branch of government accumulates unchecked authority.
Proportional representation produces multi-party systems; first-past-the-post encourages two-party systems.
Fiscal federalism allocates taxing and spending powers between national and subnational governments.
Weber's ideal-type bureaucracy achieves efficiency through specialization, hierarchy, and impersonality.
Authoritarianism uses repression, propaganda, and co-optation to maintain power without legitimate elections.
State capacity means the ability to implement policy; weak states may have formal institutions but lack capacity.
Corruption reduces investment, diverts resources, and erodes state legitimacy.
The welfare state provides social insurance against unemployment, illness, disability, and old age.
Civil society provides accountability outside formal government structures.
Policy cycle: agenda-setting, formulation, adoption, implementation, evaluation, feedback.
Affective political polarization has increased sharply in many democracies since 2000.
Decentralization transfers power to local governments and may improve responsiveness.
States of emergency expand executive power; democratic backsliding often begins under emergency justification.
International organizations like the UN, WTO, and IMF create multilateral frameworks.
PSYCHOLOGY OF HUMAN EXPERIENCE: CBT links thoughts, emotions, and behaviors; cognitive restructuring challenges distorted automatic thoughts.
Attachment theory (Bowlby, Ainsworth): secure, anxious, avoidant, and disorganized attachment patterns.
Maslow's hierarchy: physiological, safety, belonging, esteem, and self-actualization.
PERMA model (Seligman): positive emotions, engagement, relationships, meaning, achievement.
Big Five (OCEAN): openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, neuroticism.
PTSD: somatic experiencing and EMDR are evidence-based trauma therapies.
Flow states (Csikszentmihalyi): absorption at the intersection of high challenge and high skill.
Cognitive load theory: working memory is limited; instruction should minimize extraneous load.
Conformity (Asch), obedience (Milgram), and bystander effect (Darley & Latane) reveal situational power.
Neuroplasticity: the adult brain retains structural change capacity through learning and environment.
Kuebler-Ross's five stages of grief are descriptive, not prescriptive; grief is highly individual.
Resilience protective factors include social support, self-efficacy, and meaning-making.
Stereotype threat impairs performance when individuals fear confirming negative group stereotypes.
Self-determination theory: intrinsic motivation requires autonomy, competence, and relatedness.
Cognitive reappraisal changes how one thinks about a situation to regulate emotional responses.
CRAFT & TRADITIONAL SKILLS: Dovetail and mortise-and-tenon joints exploit mechanical interlock; hide glue is reversible.
Blacksmithing: iron heated to about 1100 degrees C is shaped by hammer, fuller, and hardy tools.
Weaving interlaces warp (vertical) and weft (horizontal) threads; pattern complexity increases with shaft count.
Ceramics: earthenware, stoneware, and porcelain fire at 1000, 1200, and 1300 degrees C respectively.
Vegetable-tanned leather is stiff and toolable; chrome-tanned leather is soft and water-resistant.
Glassblowing gathers molten glass at about 1200 degrees C; marvering, blowing, and tooling shape the form.
Traditional stone masonry uses lime mortar (not cement); arches and vaults transfer load by compression.
Basket weaving uses rattan, willow, and sweetgrass; patterns encode cultural identity.
Beer brewing: malting, mashing, lautering, boiling with hops, fermentation, and conditioning.
Rope-making: fibers to yarn to strands to rope; twist direction (lay) determines handling properties.
Traditional cob and adobe buildings use locally available materials for passive climate control.
Knife-making: steel selection, heat treatment, and blade geometry determine cutting performance.
Saddle stitching with two needles is stronger than machine sewing for leather goods.
Fermentation and salt inhibit pathogens while enabling beneficial organisms in food preservation.
Bookbinding: sewn signatures with boards and covering; coptic and limp vellum bindings predate hard covers.

Damascus steel was a legendary blade material made from wootz steel, prized for its wavy pattern, sharpness, and toughness; the original technique was lost and modern pattern-welded 'Damascus' folds layered steels to mimic it.
Mortise and tenon joinery fits a projecting tenon on one piece into a matching mortise cavity in another, creating a strong, ancient woodworking joint often locked with a peg.
The dovetail joint interlocks angled pins and tails so the connection resists being pulled apart, prized in drawer and cabinet making.
Blacksmithing shapes iron and steel by heating to a malleable orange-hot state and hammering; quenching hardens the metal and tempering restores toughness.
Pottery is shaped from clay then fired in a kiln; earthenware fires around 1000 C, stoneware around 1200 C, and porcelain around 1300 C, each denser and harder.
Hand weaving interlaces perpendicular warp and weft threads on a loom; the pattern of over-and-under crossings determines the fabric's structure and strength.
Tanning preserves animal hide into leather by treating it with tannins or chromium salts that stop decay and make it flexible and durable.
Traditional masonry stacks stone or brick bonded with mortar; the arch carries load in compression, letting builders span openings without a lintel.
Glassblowing shapes molten glass gathered on a blowpipe by inflating and tooling it while it is soft, exploiting glass's transition between solid and liquid.
Knife sharpening removes metal at a consistent angle (typically 15-20 degrees per side) to form a fine edge, then hones away the burr.
METAPHYSICS, MYSTICISM & OCCULT SCIENCES: Hermetic axiom 'As above, so below' from the Corpus Hermeticum posits macrocosm-microcosm correspondence.
Kabbalah: the Tree of Life (Sefirot) maps divine emanations from Ein Sof (the infinite) to manifest reality.
